title: Running

jokes.text: Sue says 'I'm sorry' reflexively then immediately corrects herself: 'Actually, I'm not sorry. I'm not sure why I just said that.'
jokes.setup: Sue begins a polite, professional phone call with an automatic apology
jokes.punchline: She immediately catches herself and admits she has no idea why she said it — a deadpan moment of self-awareness that reveals her robotic precision
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Sue's character in two sentences — hyper-efficient, socially calibrated, and yet ruthlessly honest. The joke works because it's simultaneously polite and blunt, and the self-correction is funnier than any insult. High character integration because only Sue could open with this.

jokes.text: 'I mean, I lied and everything, but it sounded true, at least.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is celebrating the VP's successful TV interview where she was called the 'no-BS VP'
jokes.punchline: Selina casually admits she lied during the interview but dismisses it because it sounded believable — the entire premise of her brand is punctured
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical line that crystallizes the show's thesis: political authenticity is performance. Only Selina could say this so casually. The juxtaposition of 'no-BS' brand and immediate admission of lying is the heart of the show's satire.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Just need six more years like today, and they will vote us POTUS.'
jokes.setup: Dan is talking about the VP's good day and future prospects
jokes.punchline: He says 'vote US POTUS' — revealing his complete conflation of his own ambition with Selina's, as if he'd be president too
jokes.explanation: The 'us' is doing quiet comic work — Dan's parasitic ambition slipping out in one word. Not the biggest laugh but character-specific and economical.

jokes.text: 'I can run that thing in a suit of armor.'
jokes.setup: Staff mention that Selina should prepare for the 10K Fun Run tomorrow
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses it with absurd bravado — the image of the VP in a suit of armor running a 10K is inherently funny, and it will be ironic given her later injury
jokes.explanation: Gets rewatch value because of the dramatic irony — she's about to walk into a glass door and injure herself. The bravado is very Selina. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Give them all donor boners.'
jokes.setup: Staff are discussing how Selina needs to charm wealthy donors at a brunch
jokes.punchline: Selina reduces political fundraising to sexual arousal in a single coinage — 'donor boners'
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable coinage that perfectly encapsulates the transactional nature of political fundraising and Selina's frank vulgarity. Maximum economy — two words that carry an entire satirical idea. Very Veep in sensibility.

jokes.text: 'Hey, why doesn't POTUS just make an apology? I mean, I did and I was only, like, suicidal for a week. Huh?'
jokes.setup: Discussion of the President's scandal and why he won't apologize
jokes.punchline: Selina casually mentions her near-suicidal state post-apology as if it's a minor inconvenience — a throwaway dark admission delivered with total breeziness
jokes.explanation: The 'Huh?' at the end is the kicker — Selina presenting suicidal ideation as a reasonable data point in a logical argument. The casualness is everything. This is Veep at its dark best: the character's emotional dysfunction weaponized for comedy without the show ever stopping to comment on it.

jokes.text: 'He's got a cave full of bats in his skull.'
jokes.setup: Discussion of why POTUS won't apologize despite the crisis
jokes.punchline: Ben offers this surrealist metaphor for the President's mental state with complete authority
jokes.explanation: A vivid, original insult delivered with Ben's characteristic weary authority. The specificity of 'a cave full of bats' elevates it above a generic crazy-person insult.

jokes.text: 'One of Gaddafi's sons. They're polling better.'
jokes.setup: Ben reveals there's a rumored challenger inside the party, and Selina asks who
jokes.punchline: Ben names Gaddafi's sons — dead Libyan dictator's offspring — as polling better than POTUS, delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of citing dead dictator's children as more electable is very Iannucci — it takes the bleak political cynicism to a logical extreme. Economical and specific.

jokes.text: 'What are you going to do for an encore? Blow the opening day pitch out of your ass?'
jokes.setup: Ben sarcastically thanks Selina for her proactive moves that have made things worse
jokes.punchline: The grotesque baseball metaphor — an 'encore' that involves a pitch blown 'out of your ass' — is vivid, aggressive, and characteristically Ben
jokes.explanation: Solid Veep insult comedy with a vivid physical image, though not quite at the level of the best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: 'We are at DEFCON fuck.'
jokes.setup: Ben is cataloguing the cascading crises — subpoenas, challengers, scandals
jokes.punchline: Military alert terminology repurposed with maximum vulgarity as the only adequate descriptor of the situation
jokes.explanation: Three words that perfectly summarize the episode's premise. Maximum economy — it says everything about the severity of the crisis, Ben's voice, and the show's sensibility. Highly quotable and instantly iconic as a formulation. Earns its high score because no other show would phrase it this way, and it's immediately understood.

jokes.text: 'Sidney Purcell, talking to him, it's like being stuck in a sewer pipe filled with dead rats.'
jokes.setup: Selina is rattling off donor flash card info to Gary, describing Sidney Purcell
jokes.punchline: The sensory specificity of the metaphor — a sewer pipe FILLED WITH DEAD RATS — is grotesque, vivid, and funnier than any standard insult
jokes.explanation: The specificity makes this — not just a sewer pipe, but one filled with dead rats. Selina's disgust with the people she has to court is made visceral. Original metaphor with high quotability.

jokes.text: 'He's got money to burn. Not that donating to your campaign is burning money.'
jokes.setup: Gary uses the idiom 'money to burn' while briefing Selina on donor Sidney Purcell
jokes.punchline: He immediately corrects himself in a panic, realizing the idiom implies donating to Selina is a waste — the correction only makes it more awkward
jokes.explanation: Classic Gary humor — tripping over himself to flatter Selina and accidentally making it worse. Character-specific and well-structured.

jokes.text: 'I know what this is all about. This is a political prick tease today.' / 'Yeah, and we're about to meet a bunch of pricks.'
jokes.setup: Selina observes they're teasing donors without committing to anything
jokes.punchline: Gary's literal echo — 'a bunch of pricks' — takes the metaphor somewhere she didn't intend, and its bluntness lands as its own joke
jokes.explanation: A neat little wordplay exchange — the double meaning activated and then extended bluntly. Competent Veep dialogue work.

jokes.text: 'The West Womb!'
jokes.setup: The team is excitedly riffing on female-centric slogans for Selina's potential presidential run ('Ovaries in the Oval Office')
jokes.punchline: Mike contributes 'The West Womb' — a portmanteau of 'The West Wing' and 'womb' — and then has to explain the anatomy to blank stares
jokes.explanation: The joke is the pun itself AND the deflating explanation that follows. Mike's enthusiasm and the room's confusion make it land twice. 'You know, like a womb. Like a woman has a womb that the baby comes...' — the trailing explanation kills the moment perfectly.

jokes.text: Selina: 'You know, my niece loves Katy Perry.' Staff: 'Just like I said, get Dan.'
jokes.setup: The team needs to make a Spotify playlist to compete with Danny Chung
jokes.punchline: Selina's out-of-touch suggestion is immediately shut down — the joke is the speed and bluntness of the dismissal
jokes.explanation: Competent but functional. The punchline relies entirely on timing and Selina's obliviousness — no particular verbal originality.

jokes.text: 'The Young Chungers.' / 'It's like you think in hashtags.'
jokes.setup: Dan is bragging about his branding for Danny Chung's demographic groups
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'you think in hashtags' is a precise, withering diagnosis of Dan's media-brained personality
jokes.explanation: 'Thinking in hashtags' is a sharp, economical characterization of a certain type of political operative — both observational and character-specific. Dan's pleased response ('You like that?') adds another layer.

jokes.text: 'Maybe we can call the over 65ers the Granny Chungs. Something. No, I'll keep working on that one.'
jokes.setup: Dan is on a roll naming demographic groups
jokes.punchline: He floats 'Granny Chungs,' immediately recognizes it doesn't quite work, but commits to workshopping it — revealing the performative nature of political branding
jokes.explanation: The self-aware failure is character-specific to Dan's relentless branding brain. Funny in a small way.

jokes.text: 'You're young, you're hip, you're hop.'
jokes.setup: Selina is asking Sue to help with the playlist, flattering her
jokes.punchline: The fractured 'hip hop' — delivered as 'hip' and 'hop' as separate adjectives — reveals Selina's complete inability to relate to youth culture
jokes.explanation: Deceptively simple. The word split of 'hip hop' into two separate compliments shows Selina has heard the phrase but doesn't understand it — a perfect small character reveal. Could only come from this character trying to seem cool. Very economical.

jokes.text: 'Your mix is my command.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks Dan to make her a playlist
jokes.punchline: Dan parodies 'your wish is my command' with the playlist context — a quick wit signifier, though minor
jokes.explanation: A competent pun, nothing more. Gets a mild chuckle.

jokes.text: Elvis Costello suggestion rejected: 'What? No, no, and no.' / 'Are you getting these all from your iPod?' / 'No. I think it's in a cloud.'
jokes.setup: The playlist discussion continues with increasingly fruitless suggestions
jokes.punchline: Sue's slightly confused 'I think it's in a cloud' captures the generational gap perfectly — the older staffer uncertain about her own technology
jokes.explanation: Mildly funny technology confusion joke — competent filler comedy.

jokes.text: 'Well, the way POTUS is going, he'll probably stumble into a war sooner or later. Chung will reenlist and hopefully get himself killed.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks if Chung is the one planning to challenge POTUS
jokes.punchline: Dan's casual strategic hope that a political rival will die in war — delivered as a logical extension of political analysis — is bracingly dark
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Dan line — the amorality is expressed through political logic, not explicit malice. He's not wishing Chung dead for personal reasons; it's just good strategy. The casualness is what makes it work.

jokes.text: 'Duchess is down. Yellow, yellow. I repeat, duchess is down. Go, Rick.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just walked into a glass door
jokes.punchline: The Secret Service activates full emergency protocol — codenames, colors, military urgency — for a lady walking into a door, deflating the grandeur of protective security
jokes.explanation: The formality of Secret Service protocol applied to mundane mishap is a classic Veep juxtaposition. 'Duchess is down' is inherently funny as a code phrase.

jokes.text: 'Don't move. We're gonna have to cut your clothes off you. I walked through a glass door before. This is what you do, okay? You're kidding. No, I was fine because I was drunk.'
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to help a shocked Selina after the glass door incident
jokes.punchline: Gary's advice is rooted in personal experience — he's done this himself, and he was only fine because he was drunk at the time. This is meant to be reassuring.
jokes.explanation: Gary offering 'I was drunk, so I was fine' as genuine medical advice is a beautiful character beat — his incompetence disguised as experience. The reveal that he has walked through a glass door himself is perfectly pathetic.

jokes.text: 'I've got some St. John's wort for pain relief if you want it. It's herbal. It's from the earth. You can take six, it won't affect you.'
jokes.setup: Selina is in pain and Gary is trying to help medically
jokes.punchline: Gary's homeopathic solution — delivered with evangelical certainty — is the seed of a running gag that pays off catastrophically when it interacts with her antidepressants
jokes.explanation: Gets rewatch bonus because this throwaway Gary moment plants the entire drug subplot. 'It's from the earth' is a wonderful idiom for Gary's credulous naturalism.

jokes.text: Mike lies to donors: 'She's stuck on a call with the Australian ambassador.' Sidney Purcell: 'The Australian ambassador? Oh, that's good. I've never heard that one before.'
jokes.setup: Mike attempts to cover for Selina with a flimsy excuse
jokes.punchline: Purcell immediately sees through it and sarcastically compliments it as a cover story he's never heard — both exposing the lie and revealing how many times he's been given cover stories
jokes.explanation: Purcell's sardonic compliment exposes the donor class's complete cynicism about political excuses. Well-structured with Purcell's follow-up 'No, it's not, actually, but you get marks for trying' piling on.

jokes.text: Mike: 'But the Vice President is in great spirits. She's buoyantly waiting to meet you all.' Purcell: 'Like I said, it's a call.'
jokes.setup: Mike is overcorrecting, pouring on enthusiasm to compensate for the weak excuse
jokes.punchline: Purcell cuts through the spin with minimal words — reminding Mike of the cover story with total contempt for the performance
jokes.explanation: Purcell's callback to his own skepticism is efficient and deadpan. The joke is entirely in the economy.

jokes.text: 'Strength is being flexible and steadfast. The living willow, not the dead oak.'
jokes.setup: We cut to Danny Chung speaking to a crowd — his political rival
jokes.punchline: His speech is pure empty inspirational word salad — 'flexible AND steadfast' is an oxymoron delivered as wisdom, followed by the pretentious tree metaphor
jokes.explanation: The incoherent profundity of Chung's speech is excellent political satire — it sounds deep while meaning nothing. 'Flexible and steadfast' is a perfect contradiction presented as insight.

jokes.text: '...and that aspiration is to one day hope to dream together.'
jokes.setup: Chung continues his inspirational speech with escalating platitudes
jokes.punchline: The stacked qualifiers — 'one day HOPE to DREAM together' — make the aspiration so attenuated it becomes meaningless. Pure empty rhetoric beautifully constructed.
jokes.explanation: This is precision satirical writing — the triple hedge 'one day hope to dream' systematically evacuates all meaning from political aspiration. Could be a real political speech. Sharp.

jokes.text: 'This is awkward. Like catching your sister's eye at an orgy.'
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy have both turned up to hear Chung's speech, both trying to assess him as a threat
jokes.punchline: Dan's orgy metaphor for mutual embarrassment is surreal, dark, and specific — and then Amy's response makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: The specificity — not 'catching someone's eye' but your SISTER'S eye, at an ORGY — makes this exceptional. The implicit family dysfunction is funnier than the orgy reference. And Amy's immediate, earnest defense of her sister ('she would want health records from everyone') fully commits to the bit, making it land twice. High quotability.

jokes.text: Amy's response: 'Well, my sister would never be at an orgy. Too uptight. She would want health records from everyone and just kill the momentum and...'
jokes.setup: Dan has used the orgy/sister metaphor
jokes.punchline: Amy doesn't reject the premise — she defends her sister's specific orgy behavior, imagining the health records request and the 'momentum killing' in vivid detail
jokes.explanation: This extension of the bit is actually funnier than the original setup. Amy's detailed imagination of her sister's orgy behavior — demanding health records, killing momentum — is character comedy gold. It reveals something true about Amy (her own control-freak tendencies projected onto her sister) while being spectacularly absurd. A rare case where the response outshines the setup.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Okay. I'm disappointed in you. This is the kind of behavior I would expect from myself. But from you? Uh-huh.'
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan have both been caught spying on Chung
jokes.punchline: Dan's self-aware condemnation — acknowledging his own bad behavior as the baseline and being disappointed Amy has met it — is a perfect Dan line
jokes.explanation: The structure is elegant — Dan has zero self-awareness about his hypocrisy while being perfectly self-aware about his own standards. 'This is the kind of behavior I would expect from myself' is the key line.

jokes.text: Dan: 'So we both jump together. You know, Butch and Sundance.' Amy: 'Don't they both die?' Dan: 'No, not when they jump. They die at the end.'
jokes.setup: Dan proposes they team up by comparing them to Butch Cassidy and Sundance
jokes.punchline: Amy correctly identifies that this is not a good metaphor — they die — and Dan's pedantic clarification that the death comes later doesn't help his case
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed little exchange — the metaphor is fatally undermined by Amy's literal reading, and Dan's desperate repair attempt makes it worse. Clean structure.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, that woman has become a living metaphor of her own career.'
jokes.setup: Sue has told Dan that Selina walked through a glass door (literally), and he keeps asking if it's code
jokes.punchline: Sue's dry observation that the literal glass door is also a perfect metaphor for Selina's political career — stated with total flatness
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. Sue's observation works on three levels: (1) it's a genuine metaphor for Selina's career obstacles, (2) it's delivered by the most deadpan character in the show, (3) the double meaning of 'glass ceiling' and 'glass door' is doing quiet work. The complete flatness of delivery — as if this is just a factual observation — makes it exceptional. High quotability, rewatch bonus for the depth.

jokes.text: 'I feel like one of those old used footballs that they fixed up for the kids at an orphanage.'
jokes.setup: Selina is recovering from the glass door injury in her hotel room
jokes.punchline: This specific, oddly compassionate self-image — not just a used football, but one repaired FOR ORPHANS — is a perfect Selina combination of self-pity and weirdly generous analogy
jokes.explanation: The specificity is everything. Not just a used football — one fixed up for orphans, which implies it's been through multiple owners and is still being passed down to those with the least to expect. The image is simultaneously pathetic and oddly noble, perfectly capturing Selina's grandiose self-pity. Highly quotable, original simile.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Unless you want to go down there and sing them a chorus of Look At Me, I'm Sandra Dee, you can't go downstairs.'
jokes.setup: Selina insists she can't cancel her destiny and must meet the donors despite being wrapped in a robe with cuts
jokes.punchline: Mike's specific pop culture reference — 'Look At Me I'm Sandra Dee' from Grease, implying Selina in her bathrobe looks like a frumpy ingenue — is unexpected and specific
jokes.explanation: The reference is specific enough to be funny — Mike's cultural touchstone being a 1978 musical film is itself a character note. Decent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'I guess we just follow the trail of her blood and Gary's tears.'
jokes.setup: Staff is trying to find where Selina is in the hotel
jokes.punchline: The pairing of 'blood' (physical consequence) with 'Gary's tears' (Gary's emotional devastation at Selina's pain) captures both the crisis and Gary's character in one image
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy: one clause tells us the physical situation (blood from glass) and another tells us Gary's emotional response (tears). Both are funny. The parallelism is beautiful — two different types of liquid marking the trail. Highly quotable, original construction. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: 'I think I got that off a Catholic schoolgirl once.'
jokes.setup: Gary mentions he gave Selina St. John's wort; someone asks why she can't take painkillers
jokes.punchline: Dan's offhand comment about acquiring St. John's wort from a Catholic schoolgirl implies an entire unsavory backstory that is never elaborated on
jokes.explanation: The 'once' is doing heavy lifting — it implies this is a repeatable scenario while also being a throwaway. Dan's implication is both funny and darkly character-appropriate. Economy is high.

jokes.text: Gary: 'it can react badly with the body if she's already on immunosuppressants, beta-blockers, or antidepressants.' / Amy and Dan simultaneously: 'Oh, okay, fine. She's not on any of those.' / Beat / 'Well, she is on antidepressants.'
jokes.setup: The doctor is listing what St. John's wort can't be combined with; Amy and Dan quickly dismiss the concern
jokes.punchline: Amy privately concedes to Dan that Selina is in fact on antidepressants — a revelation they both silently absorb and don't act on fast enough
jokes.explanation: The structural setup is excellent — the rapid reassurance followed by the immediate quiet revelation, with both Amy and Dan's calm 'Yep' response. The joke is in the complete absence of urgency. Rewatch bonus because this moment plants the St. John's wort/antidepressants running gag.

jokes.text: Gary: 'Who has the presidential suite?' Selina: 'I heard Russell Crowe.' Gary: 'Russell Crowe, Gladiator.' Selina: '"I'll be back."'
jokes.setup: Selina and Gary are bonding in the hotel room, discussing who got the presidential suite
jokes.punchline: Selina misattributes the Terminator's famous line to Russell Crowe / Gladiator — revealing her casual cultural confusion
jokes.explanation: The misquote joke — attributing 'I'll be back' to Gladiator/Russell Crowe — is a solid character beat. Selina's cultural glibness is always good for a beat.

jokes.text: Gary reveals his backstory: grew up in Birmingham, Alabama; father 'always wanted a man for a son'; parents are celebrating their 40th anniversary. Selina: 'Can I come?'
jokes.setup: Selina says Gary is a mystery man and asks about his background — a rare moment of genuine human curiosity
jokes.punchline: Gary mentions his parents' big party, Selina immediately invites herself — because everything orbits around her, even a private family moment
jokes.explanation: Gets rewatch bonus because this setup pays off in the episode's final joke. Selina's genuine emotional warmth and her instinctive narcissism coexist in one line. Gary's hope and vulnerability make the eventual callback painful and funny.

jokes.text: Selina high on St. John's wort/antidepressants: 'God, I hope Iran drops a nuke on DC. Wouldn't that solve a lot of issues?'
jokes.setup: Selina is in a euphoric, drugged-up state and is casually riffing on world events
jokes.punchline: The VP of the United States cheerfully wishes for a nuclear strike on the capital city — because it would solve her political problems
jokes.explanation: The casual geopolitical nihilism fits the show perfectly. The qualifier 'wouldn't that solve a lot of issues' frames a nuclear catastrophe as a project management solution. The drugs make it read as genuine euphoric honesty.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Except Kent wouldn't go anywhere. He'd still be around. He's like an undead cockroach with his... He's got that blank look. I bet he doesn't even have a come face.'
jokes.setup: Selina riffs on who would survive the nuclear strike she just wished for
jokes.punchline: Escalating from 'undead cockroach' to speculating about Kent's orgasm face is a surreal and deranged character observation, especially from the VP in a drugged state
jokes.explanation: The escalation from cockroach metaphor to 'come face' speculation is perfectly unhinged. 'I bet he doesn't even have a come face' is a brilliantly specific insult that implies Kent is so emotionless he doesn't register pleasure. Highly quotable, original.

jokes.text: 'Can you imagine fucking that guy?' — Amy's appalled reaction
jokes.setup: Selina has been escalating her drugged commentary to Kent's sex life
jokes.punchline: Selina directly asks Amy to imagine having sex with their colleague — Amy's 'Whoa' reaction is the punchline
jokes.explanation: The cringe lands primarily through Amy's reaction. The question itself is the shock, but it's the performance that carries it.

jokes.text: Selina immediately pivots to: 'Amy, you have such pretty blonde hair. Doesn't she? So pretty.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just horrified Amy with the Kent sex question
jokes.punchline: The drug-induced topic switch — from graphic sexual speculation to childlike compliments about hair — is a perfect tonal whiplash
jokes.explanation: The pivot is perfect — Selina's drug-addled brain jumping from 'can you imagine fucking Kent' to 'you have such pretty hair' captures high-functioning intoxication beautifully. The doubled 'so pretty' is the kicker. Excellent structure and economy.

jokes.text: Mike to donors: 'Unfortunately, the Vice President is still stuck on a phone call with Australia, so she's going to have to postpone...' Purcell: 'Mike, you know, in the southern hemisphere, the bullshit actually flows the other way. Did you know that?'
jokes.setup: Mike attempts the same Australia cover story a second time
jokes.punchline: Purcell's science metaphor for Mike's bullshit — referencing the Coriolis effect as a comment on spin direction — is both a callback and an original escalation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The Coriolis-effect-as-bullshit-metaphor is precise, original, and perfectly suited to Purcell's character — he's a sophisticated man who enjoys tormenting Mike with his own cleverness. 'The bullshit actually flows the other way' is an instant quotable. Rewatch bonus because it rewards knowledge of the earlier exchange.

jokes.text: 'I guess it's funny because we've all done it at one point.' [MAN: 'I haven't.'] 'I pushed someone through a plate glass door once. That was fucking hilarious.'
jokes.setup: Purcell learns Selina walked through a glass door and tries to normalize it
jokes.punchline: The unnamed man's 'I haven't' deflates the normalization, then Purcell's own glass door story — where he PUSHED someone through it and found it hilarious — paints him as a sociopath
jokes.explanation: Three-beat structure: normalization attempt → deflation → unexpected revelation. Purcell's casual violence ('I pushed someone through a plate glass door once') followed by 'that was fucking hilarious' reveals him as exactly the type of person who'd burn money on a political campaign. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: 'You are liaison to the VP, right? That's your liaison d'étre?'
jokes.setup: Purcell is questioning Jonah about his role
jokes.punchline: 'Liaison d'être' — a pun on 'raison d'être' (reason for being) replacing 'raison' with 'liaison' — is a French-tinged wordplay that fits Purcell's elevated, contemptuous register
jokes.explanation: An excellent pun because it works on two levels: it's a genuine witticism AND it characterizes Purcell as someone who would deploy French puns to demean an inferior. The wordplay is clean and economical. One of the episode's cleverest verbal constructions.

jokes.text: 'I mean, that is tantamount to calling the President Jonad.' Purcell: 'No, it's not. He's the President, you're Jonad.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is complaining that being called 'Jonad' is disrespectful to his position
jokes.punchline: Purcell's flat correction — the President is the President, Jonah is Jonad, these are not equivalent — is delivered as a logical statement with devastating effect
jokes.explanation: The logic is irrefutable. Purcell simply states the fact — the President and Jonah are not comparable — and the flatness is the comedy. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'Hello there, Susan.' Sue: 'Name's not Susan, it's Sue, Jonad.' Jonah: 'Okay, and my name isn't Jonad!' Sue: 'It's Jonah.' Ben: 'No one here is disrespecting you, Jonad.'
jokes.setup: Jonah attempts to assert authority over Sue by using the wrong name
jokes.punchline: Sue immediately Jonad-s him back; Jonah's outrage at his nickname is undermined by his own behavior; Ben's dismissal uses the nickname again
jokes.explanation: A well-orchestrated three-person exchange. The irony of Jonah using the wrong name while complaining about his own wrong name is solid. Sue's precision and Ben's casual repetition both work.

jokes.text: Sue's careful double-negative dance: 'The Vice President was not at a brunch with campaign donors.' / Ben: 'I would hope that she was not. Otherwise, I'm not sure if I wouldn't be within my rights not to launch a drone strike right now.' / Sue: 'I got lost in the double negative, sir.'
jokes.setup: Ben tries to get Sue to confirm where Selina was through a deniability-preserving exchange of double negatives
jokes.punchline: Ben's triple negative drone strike threat is so convoluted that even the hyper-precise Sue loses track, and she says so flatly
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest verbal constructions. The whole exchange satirizes political deniability through grammatical absurdism — both characters speaking in negatives to avoid saying anything. Ben's 'I wouldn't be within my rights not to launch a drone strike' is deliberately impenetrable. Sue breaking character to admit she's lost is the perfect payoff. High character integration — requires both Ben's controlled aggression and Sue's precision.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Cut your face off and give it to the VP if you have to.'
jokes.setup: Ben is ordering Jonah to get Selina to talk to the press
jokes.punchline: The face-cutting instruction — meaning 'do whatever it takes' expressed in grotesque body horror — is classic Ben hyperbole
jokes.explanation: Ben's instructions expressed in body horror metaphors are a signature of his character. The specific instruction to 'cut your face off and give it to the VP' to cover her cuts is absurd and vivid.

jokes.text: Gary and Selina vocalizing and dancing together about going to the anniversary party
jokes.setup: They've just confirmed the party plan
jokes.punchline: Both of them break into wordless vocalizing and dance moves about the upcoming party — the VP and her bag man sharing a completely unguarded moment
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in the visual — inferred from the transcript. Their shared joy is funny because it's utterly incongruous with the political chaos around them. Character-dependent.

jokes.text: Gary and Selina vocalizing dance moves together / 'Dance on.' / 'I'm really excited for that party.'
jokes.setup: Gary has accepted Selina's invitation to his parents' anniversary party — they're sharing a genuinely warm moment.
jokes.punchline: The sight of the VP and her bag-carrier doing verbal dance warm-ups together is inherently absurd, and the warmth of 'Dance on' makes the later callback (when she forgets entirely) much more painful.
jokes.explanation: Primarily a setup for the devastating callback. On its own, it's sweet-absurd; in context, it's cringe comedy because we know Selina's drug-addled promises are worthless. The rewatch bonus is high.

jokes.text: Amy: 'She's out of her fucking mind.' Gary: 'She's coming to my parents' 40th wedding anniversary.' Amy: 'Yeah, exactly, Gary. She's out of her fucking mind.'
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan react to the dancing
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'she's out of her fucking mind' is said twice, but the second time it refers to both the drugs AND the party plan — Gary's happy acceptance of what is clearly an insane invitation
jokes.explanation: The structural repetition with shifted meaning is excellent. First usage: Selina is drugged. Second usage, after Gary's innocent explanation: yes, and she's ALSO invited herself to your parents' party. Gary's missing the point completes the joke.

jokes.text: Gary: 'It's herbal. It's from the earth.' Dan: 'So are mushrooms, dipshit.' Gary: 'Okay, that's illegal drugs.'
jokes.setup: Gary defends the St. John's wort with his 'it's from the earth' mantra again
jokes.punchline: Dan points out that psychedelic mushrooms are also from the earth; Gary's response — that mushrooms are 'illegal drugs' — misses the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. The logical trap — 'so are mushrooms' — is a perfect counter to Gary's herbal philosophy, and his retreat to legality (missing the point that BOTH are affecting Selina) is character-perfect. The exchange is economical and structurally clean. Gary's 'that's illegal drugs' as a distinction without a difference lands hard.

jokes.text: Dan buys Mike's boat while Selina is clearly intoxicated: 'Shake on it, Magic Mikey.' / Mike: 'You just got a boat.' Dan: 'And I just got a vote.'
jokes.setup: Dan exploits the chaotic situation to buy Mike's boat at a discount, securing Mike's loyalty
jokes.punchline: Dan transparently transacting loyalty — 'I just got a vote' — while Selina is deliriously unaware captures both Dan's opportunism and the show's transactional politics thesis
jokes.explanation: The transaction happens in real-time, with the drug-addled VP as witness and the exchange stated explicitly: boat for vote. The casual corruption is perfectly rendered. 'Magic Mikey' is also a good character beat. High character integration for Dan.

jokes.text: Amy: 'Look, she is fuckin' high right now.' (beat) / [sound of Selina urinating] / [Selina laughing]
jokes.setup: The team is trying to decide how to handle the Selina situation as press vans appear outside
jokes.punchline: Amy's declaration that Selina is high is immediately illustrated by the sound of Selina urinating, followed by her laughing to herself
jokes.explanation: A show-don't-tell comedy moment that works perfectly in the single-camera format. Amy states Selina is high; immediately the sound design provides evidence. The VP urinating and laughing to herself is both undignified and strangely poignant. The economy of this joke is exceptional — it needs no dialogue to land. Strong structure: statement → illustration → grace note laugh.

jokes.text: 'You didn't happen to notice that the Veep is tripping balls?'
jokes.setup: Staff reconvene outside the VP's room
jokes.punchline: Amy's clinical delivery of 'tripping balls' — slang for extreme intoxication — applied to the Vice President is funny through register collision: official gravity meets street vocabulary
jokes.explanation: Efficient register collision — 'tripping balls' in Amy's precise deadpan mouth applied to the VP is funny. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Gary gave her something that is reacting with her antidepressants and turning her into Julie Andrews.'
jokes.setup: Amy is explaining Selina's altered state to the arriving staff
jokes.punchline: Julie Andrews as the reference point for drug-induced euphoric wholesomeness is unexpected and specific — it implies singing, twirling, saying nice things about people's hair
jokes.explanation: Julie Andrews as the drug metaphor is inspired — it captures exactly what we've seen (Selina being sweet about Amy's hair, joyfully dancing about the party). The reference is both unexpected and precisely accurate to what she's doing. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'What if we pump her stomach?' / 'I've had my stomach pumped before. I think I can figure it out.'
jokes.setup: The team is discussing how to sober up Selina quickly
jokes.punchline: Someone's volunteering to pump the VP's stomach based on personal experience with the procedure — revealing a colorful past
jokes.explanation: The casual past personal experience with stomach pumping is dark and funny. The confidence ('I think I can figure it out') from non-medical experience is the joke.

jokes.text: 'You just broke his brain, Amy. Jesus, look at his stupid gaping mouth. Let's put stuff in it.'
jokes.setup: Amy has just explained to Jonah the full situation (glass door + drug reaction)
jokes.punchline: Dan's gleeful suggestion that they put things in Jonah's open mouth while he's stunned is a perfect expression of his sadistic enjoyment of Jonah's confusion
jokes.explanation: Dan's cruelty toward Jonah is a recurring motif, but the specificity of 'let's put stuff in it' (referring to his open mouth) is fresh and funny. The 'broke his brain' metaphor is also good.

jokes.text: Jonah makes his big decision pronouncement: 'Ca-fuckin'-pisce?' — then accidentally calls someone by the wrong name ('What in the hell is your problem, Andrew?')
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to assert authority by making a decisive statement about the press plan
jokes.punchline: 'Ca-fuckin'-pisce' as a power move, followed immediately by not knowing the name of the person he's talking to — a perfect Jonah sequence of faux-confidence and incompetence
jokes.explanation: Could only be Jonah — the Italian mob loanword deployed for authority immediately undercut by not knowing someone's name. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I can even say your name the other way...' [pause] 'Haney.' [longer pause — the room processes this]
jokes.setup: Jonah has called the wrong person 'Andrew' and is trying to recover by demonstrating he knows their actual name
jokes.punchline: He reverses the letters: 'Haney.' The dramatic pause before and after makes it worse. The name reversal as proof of competence is deranged.
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode. The setup is perfect: Jonah has called someone 'Andrew' and claims he knows their name — then reverses the syllables as proof. The long pause before 'Haney' gives it room to breathe. The logic is genuinely surreal: Jonah thinks saying the name backwards demonstrates competence. There's no explanation, no punchline — the word 'Haney.' is the entire joke. Could ONLY be Jonah. This is Veep joke construction at its peak: it's absurdist, character-specific, and it hangs in the air. The 'yawns' stage direction immediately after suggests the room doesn't react, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Jonah immediately falls asleep/snores after his 'I handled that pretty well' declaration
jokes.setup: Jonah has declared himself satisfied with his leadership
jokes.punchline: He immediately passes out — either exhausted or just checked out — while the crisis continues around him
jokes.explanation: The snoring is the perfect punctuation to his self-congratulation. Very economical — no words needed after 'Gotta say, I think I handled that pretty well.' The snore is the punchline.

jokes.text: 'There's something so sexy about being in a hotel in the middle of the day, right?' Gary: 'Yep, four people to one toilet. You can hear everything that hits the water.'
jokes.setup: Selina makes a vaguely romantic observation about midday hotel stays
jokes.punchline: Gary's response — the acoustic intimacy of four people sharing one hotel toilet — perfectly deflates the romantic register with the most unromantic possible elaboration
jokes.explanation: Gary's response is a perfect anti-romantic puncture. 'You can hear everything that hits the water' is both disgusting and oddly specific — implying Gary is very conscious of this. The structural contrast between Selina's romantic preamble and Gary's toilet acoustics is flawless. High character integration — Gary's total social unawareness.

jokes.text: Amy on the phone to Gary about clothes: 'Just get back here.' Gary: 'Amy, Amy, Amy. Give me a number between one and four, please.' Amy: 'Four.' Gary: 'I think I prefer three.' Amy: 'Fucking three, then.' Gary: 'Actually, four's good.'
jokes.setup: Amy tells Gary to just get back with clothes; Gary needs Amy to validate his choice with a number
jokes.punchline: Gary rejects Amy's answer, then rejects his own preference, then loops back — the exchange perfectly captures his inability to make decisions while burdening others with his indecision
jokes.explanation: Classic Gary cringe comedy — the clothes selection loop reveals him as someone who has no internal compass whatsoever and must route all decisions through someone else, then second-guess them. The structure is a perfect spiral.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'Do I need to draw you a line graph, Jonah? More time equals less cuckoo.' / 'We need to take a cold dump on the speculation.' / 'the ring is a hat-free zone. No berets, no Stetsons, no beanies...' Amy: 'Why are you just listing hats?'
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to make the strategic case for acting quickly on the speculation
jokes.punchline: His hat metaphor for political announcements spirals into him just listing hat types, and Amy calls it out with the most efficient possible question
jokes.explanation: Jonah's metaphor ('throw your hat in the ring') metastasizes into a hat inventory. Amy's 'Why are you just listing hats?' is the most efficient possible puncture — four words that perfectly express her contempt. The combination of Jonah's verbal sprawl and Amy's surgical dismissal is a perfect comedy machine.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'We need to take a cold dump on the speculation.' / 'We need to send the message that the ring is a hat-free zone. No berets, no Stetsons, no beanies...' / Amy: 'Why are you just listing hats?'
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to articulate the political strategy for quashing presidential challenger speculation
jokes.punchline: His metaphor (throwing a hat into a ring) gets literalized and then he begins listing hat types, apparently not realizing he's doing it; Amy calls him out
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Jonah moments. The escalating hat list — 'no berets, no Stetsons, no beanies' — has perfect escalating specificity. Jonah has lost the thread of his own metaphor and descended into taxonomy. 'Why are you just listing hats?' is Amy at her most economically devastating. The joke requires the listener to realize what's happening a beat after Jonah, then laugh at Amy's articulation of what everyone was thinking. High character integration because this is only possible with Jonah.

jokes.text: Amy: 'Yeah, in 700 days of working with us, you've been right once.' Jonah: 'Sit like a gentleman, please?'
jokes.setup: Jonah correctly predicted something would happen and is self-satisfied
jokes.punchline: Amy's precise accounting — 700 days, one correct call — is devastating; Jonah's only response is to ask someone to sit more properly, ignoring the insult entirely
jokes.explanation: The specific number '700 days' makes the insult feel data-driven. Jonah's pivot to seat posture is perfect — he either doesn't process Amy's line or has no response. Both interpretations are funny.

jokes.text: Reporter asks about the run; VP: 'I fully intend to run.' — the room hears it as presidential announcement. Ben: 'Oh, no, that's a fucking wire brush to my hemorrhoids.'
jokes.setup: Selina answers a press question about the Fun Run with her running phrase
jokes.punchline: Ben's hemorrhoid metaphor for political discomfort — specifically a wire brush — is one of his best lines. The physical specificity is excruciating.
jokes.explanation: The hemorrhoid metaphor is already characteristically Ben, but the WIRE BRUSH specificity elevates it to something genuinely great. It tells us everything about Ben's exhausted, physically deteriorating relationship with politics. Original, character-specific, quotable. High marks on all dimensions.

jokes.text: Reporter: 'Were there any mitigating circumstances? Was there carpeting or...' / Mike: 'Yes.' / Selina: 'Yes.' / Mike: 'There was.' / Selina: 'Yes, it has been confirmed that there was carpeting there.'
jokes.setup: Press conference after Selina's glass door incident; reporters are asking questions
jokes.punchline: A reporter, apparently trying to be generous, asks if there was carpeting (as a mitigating factor); Selina and Mike enthusiastically confirm it, treating 'carpeting' as an exculpatory fact in a formal government press statement
jokes.explanation: A beautifully absurd press conference moment. The reporter's question is already funny (carpeting as a mitigating factor in VP clumsiness), but Selina and Mike seizing on it — 'it has been confirmed that there was carpeting' — as official exoneration is the real joke. Political spin applied to the most trivial possible fact. Strong structure: setup (reporter throws them a lifeline) → immediate, desperate grasp of that lifeline by both.

jokes.text: Purcell to reporter: 'So she's either crazy or she's starting a presidential campaign.' Reporter: 'Yeah, I'd say the two are synonymous.'
jokes.setup: The press interprets Selina's 'I intend to run' as political
jokes.punchline: 'The two are synonymous' — running for president and being crazy — is the show's central thesis delivered as a throwaway
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire delivered with economy. 'Synonymous' as a one-word bridge between insanity and presidential ambition is the whole show in one exchange. Not deeply original but very well executed.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Fix it, or I'll liaise your balls to your desk.' / Jonah: 'Yes, sir. And thank you for your continued...' Ben hangs up.
jokes.setup: Ben calls Jonah to fix the 'I intend to run' problem.
jokes.punchline: Ben's threat ('liaise your balls to your desk') inverts Jonah's job title into a torture method; Jonah's reflexive 'thank you for your continued—' as Ben hangs up is perfect.
jokes.explanation: Ben weaponizes Jonah's own job description as a threat ('liaise your balls') which is creative insult construction. Jonah's instinct to thank Ben mid-threat is the joke's perfect button — pure conditioned sycophancy. One of the better Jonah-Ben exchanges.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'She can't do the Fun Run.' Amy: 'She can barely do a convincing walk at the moment.' Dan: 'Can and will.' Amy: 'Can't and won't.' Dan: 'Now go up and screw a pillow. The room's paid for.'
jokes.setup: A three-way argument about whether Selina can do the Fun Run
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'go screw a pillow — the room's paid for' as a dismissal of Jonah is unexpectedly crude and brilliant, ending the debate with misdirection
jokes.explanation: The exchange structure is good — 'can and will' / 'can't and won't' — but the punchline is Dan's ejection of Jonah with the pillow-screwing suggestion. The practical addition 'the room's paid for' as justification is the extra twist that makes it memorable.

jokes.text: The Taiwan cartoon: anime-style news report showing Selina walking into a glass door, everyone laughing including Selina
jokes.setup: Mike announces 'this just in from Taiwan' — a foreign news animation of the glass door incident
jokes.punchline: The entire team (and a loopy Selina) watches a cartoon recreation of her humiliation and laughs, with Selina saying 'I love cartoons'
jokes.explanation: The Taiwanese CGI news animation joke (a real-world cultural reference to NMA News) is a perfect visual gag and cultural satire. The meta-humor of watching cartoon Selina fall while real Selina laughs — because she's high — is layered comedy. 'I love cartoons' captures her drug state perfectly. High impact score for the visual alone.

jokes.text: 'They're asking if you're up to the job, ma'am.' Selina: 'Am I up to the job? Ooh, jeez... Seriously, I am totally overqualified for this job.'
jokes.setup: Staff warns Selina that her health delay is causing speculation about her fitness
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to 'are you up to the job' is not to reassure — it's to assert overqualification, which is both defensive and accidentally honest about her contempt for the VP role
jokes.explanation: The question is about fitness; her answer reveals her real feelings about the position. 'Overqualified' as a response to a health question is pure Selina — it says more than she intends.

jokes.text: Flowers from Secretary Maddox: 'Good luck with your Fun Run. Try not to swear when the starter pistol goes off.' / Selina: 'He's just a varicose dick vein. I can't stand that guy.'
jokes.setup: Selina receives a passive-aggressive 'good luck' note from a rival
jokes.punchline: 'Varicose dick vein' — a compound insult that's anatomically vivid and specifically contemptuous — is one of the episode's best insult coinages
jokes.explanation: A genuinely original insult construction. Not just 'dick' but a 'varicose dick vein' — the varicose modifier implies bulging, ugly, and slightly diseased. The medical specificity elevates it far above a generic obscenity. Maximum quotability. Pure Selina.

jokes.text: Ben: 'I went to a Mexican karaoke restaurant, and I did Hallelujah by Leonard Cohen. I changed the "hallelujah" to "jalapeño."'
jokes.setup: Ben is apparently stress-relieving by karaoke between crises
jokes.punchline: Ben doing Leonard Cohen's 'Hallelujah' with the word changed to 'jalapeño' at a Mexican karaoke bar is an image so specific and strange that it's very funny
jokes.explanation: This joke works on multiple levels: (1) Ben choosing the most somber possible song for karaoke, (2) going to a Mexican restaurant specifically, (3) the jalapeño substitution being both thematically apt (Mexican venue) and phonetically close enough to work. The image of Ben dead-eyed performing this is a vivid character beat. Highly quotable, original.

jokes.text: Jonah's response: 'Oh, I can imagine that was very funny, sir.'
jokes.setup: Ben has shared the karaoke story with evident pride
jokes.punchline: Jonah's response — 'I can IMAGINE that was very funny' — implies he wasn't there and is performatively validating something he's not sure how to respond to. The hedging ('I can imagine') is inadvertently honest.
jokes.explanation: A subtle Jonah incompetence beat — the social sycophancy accidentally expressing doubt. Minor but well-placed.

jokes.text: Ben on Jonah: 'You don't get the complexity. You're the world's biggest single-cell organism.'
jokes.setup: Jonah says he thinks the committee vote will be fine
jokes.punchline: 'World's biggest single-cell organism' — a biological metaphor for intellectual simplicity that implies Jonah has not evolved past amoeba level
jokes.explanation: 'World's biggest single-cell organism' is an original and highly specific insult — not just calling him stupid but suggesting he hasn't achieved multicellular life. The biological register makes it funnier than a generic insult. Ben's delivery would be devastating.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Like Sophie's Choice choices, except more important because it's gonna be about me.'
jokes.setup: Selina warns that difficult choices are coming regarding POTUS
jokes.punchline: She references Sophie's Choice — a Holocaust narrative about impossible moral choices — and immediately clarifies they'll be MORE important because they concern her
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's top moments. The Sophie's Choice reference establishes the highest possible moral stakes, and the immediate qualification — 'more important because it's about me' — is the most perfectly Selina sentence possible. The narcissism is the joke; her complete sincerity makes it land. High character integration (could only be Selina), high originality, high quotability. Earns its 8.5.

jokes.text: Dan and Amy watching Selina on stage: 'She is definitely wearing too much makeup.' 'She looks like Marcel Marceau.' 'He couldn't tell the difference between real glass and air either.'
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy observe Selina's excessive makeup at the Fun Run starting line
jokes.punchline: The Marcel Marceau comparison is good; the callback — 'he couldn't tell the difference between real glass and air either' — connecting the famous mime to the glass door incident, is brilliant
jokes.explanation: The best callback in the episode. Marcel Marceau as a makeup comparison is already witty, but the callback to the glass door — 'he couldn't tell the difference between real glass and air either' — is a multi-layered joke: (1) the mime connection to invisible walls, (2) the callback to the glass door, (3) the perfect structural elegance of the metaphor. The line rewards attention. 'Come on, that's fuckin' funny' from the other character confirms the writers know it's a top joke. Among the best in the episode.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Did we warn her one of the Get Moving ambassadors was a one-legged veteran?' / 'He's behind her. She's never gonna see him.' / 'Does it look good, her beating a one-legged guy like that?'
jokes.setup: Selina is charging ahead in the Fun Run
jokes.punchline: The team realizes they're in a PR disaster — the VP is beating a disabled veteran in a race — and must manage it strategically
jokes.explanation: The scenario is pure Veep — every situation has a PR angle, even beating a disabled veteran is a strategic consideration. The team's clinical assessment ('does it look good, her beating a one-legged guy') rather than any moral concern is darkly funny.

jokes.text: Dan: 'The key is we make it look like she could beat the guy, but then she lets him win.' / 'Yeah, a win-win by losing. That's good.'
jokes.setup: The team strategizes about the one-legged veteran race situation
jokes.punchline: 'A win-win by losing' — Dan's immediate coinage of a strategic framework for deliberate defeat — encapsulates political spin at its most absurd
jokes.explanation: 'Win-win by losing' is an instantly usable political coinage — Dan at his strategic-spin best. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: Gary tries to stop Selina: 'No, your face looks fine. You're beating a disabled guy.' Selina: 'Oh, come on. Don't be so hard on yourself, Gary.'
jokes.setup: Gary tells Selina she's beating a disabled guy and should slow down
jokes.punchline: Selina mishears 'disabled guy' as a reference to Gary himself — and tells him not to be so hard on himself — suggesting she thinks Gary is the disabled one, or just mishearing entirely
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed misdirection: Gary delivers urgent strategic information and Selina hears personal self-deprecation. 'Don't be so hard on yourself, Gary' is a stunningly good misfire that works because it's plausible (Gary IS self-deprecating) and also deeply inadvertent. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Gary: 'I can't stop her. She's unstoppable.'
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to slow Selina down mid-race
jokes.punchline: Gary's helpless declaration that the VP is 'unstoppable' — delivered in a panicked, winded state — is both literally and metaphorically accurate
jokes.explanation: A minor beat, but the breathless delivery context makes it funnier than the line alone.

jokes.text: Ben on the phone during the race crisis: 'Burn everything incriminating, including this building. Burn all the White House pets and then yourselves. Burn yourselves first.'
jokes.setup: Ben delivers urgent instructions during an impeachment crisis
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'burn incriminating documents' to 'burn the building' to 'burn the pets' to 'burn yourselves' — with the amendment 'burn yourselves FIRST' — is a perfect comic escalation
jokes.explanation: Exceptional escalation joke. Each step logically follows from Ben's nihilism — documents, then property, then living things (pets!), then staff — with the final amendment 'burn yourselves FIRST' adding the twist that he barely distinguishes between pets and people. The pets detail is what makes it great. Among the top 3 jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Dan and Amy betting on whether Jonah will listen to Dan or Amy: Amy: 'So which one of us do you think Andre the Giant Jagoff is gonna obey?' Dan: 'I'm surprised he gets to work without being hit by a car or punched in the mouth.'
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy are competing to control Jonah's instructions to Selina
jokes.punchline: Dan's assessment of Jonah's daily survival — 'surprised he gets to work without being hit by a car or punched' — implies Jonah is so annoying that the world should be naturally injuring him
jokes.explanation: Dan's Jonah-insults are a reliable comedy engine. 'Andre the Giant Jagoff' (Amy) and the car/mouth prediction (Dan) are both good. The latter implies the universe should be punishing Jonah more than it does.

jokes.text: Selina (panting, mid-race): 'Cut to the chase, Amy. I'm being overtaken by a banana.'
jokes.setup: Amy is trying to give Selina detailed message guidance while Selina is racing
jokes.punchline: The information that a person in a banana costume is overtaking the VP mid-race, delivered with complete urgency, is absurdist gold
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top lines. The banana is both absurdly specific and perfectly credible for a Fun Run. Selina treating 'I'm being overtaken by a banana' as urgent information while Amy is discussing geopolitical strategy is perfectly structured. Nine words that earn a huge laugh. Iconic economy.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Yeah, I'm not gonna get beaten by a banana.'
jokes.setup: Mike has told Selina it's a 'caption contest waiting to happen' if she ties with the banana
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat assertion — 'I'm not gonna get beaten by a banana' — delivered as a point of dignity, is a perfect line. This is her priority.
jokes.explanation: The callback and escalation from the banana mention. The line works because of the gravity Selina gives it — this is a matter of her dignity. Seven words that perfectly capture Selina's character: she may be covered in scabs, high on drugs, and managing an impeachment crisis, but she WILL NOT lose to a banana. Excellent economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Post-race: Selina says she's 'done' — the team thinks she means tired. She means: 'I am done. I am done with all of it. The vice presidency... Forget it.'
jokes.setup: Selina says 'I am done' while panting after the race
jokes.punchline: What sounds like exhaustion is actually a political declaration — she's quitting the vice presidency. The misdirection is the gap between physical and political meaning.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is clean and contextually earned. The joke is the gap between what Amy thinks she's hearing (physical exhaustion) and what Selina means (political decision). Selina's specificity about the VP role makes it land as both serious and funny.

jokes.text: Selina post-race epiphany: 'I smell like a hobo's craphole.'
jokes.setup: Selina, exhausted after the run and increasingly clear-eyed, is having a moment of existential political reckoning
jokes.punchline: Amid her grand political declarations ('I'm not gonna run with him in two years'), she notes she smells like a hobo's craphole — the absurd physical detail amid the political drama
jokes.explanation: 'Hobo's craphole' is a very specific and evocative image that Selina drops into the middle of a significant political moment. The juxtaposition of her grand strategic thinking with her present physical state (scabs, body odor) is the joke. 'Craphole' as a word has a particular comic texture — almost childish, which makes it funnier in Selina's mouth.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I smell like a hobo's craphole.'
jokes.setup: Selina is reflecting on how she looks and feels post-race
jokes.punchline: The VP of the United States describing herself as smelling like a hobo's craphole — with absolute certainty and no self-pity — is a vivid, unpretentious self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Vivid, specific, and funny. The compound noun 'hobo's craphole' is original. Selina's matter-of-fact delivery would make it land hard.

jokes.text: Selina, mid-political-declaration: 'Hey, do you know what my time was, by the way? Like an eight-minute mile. That.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just delivered what sounds like a major political declaration — she's finished, she won't be POTUS's VP, she'll come back in six years
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots to asking about her race time, revealing that even in her most 'profound' moments, Selina is still Selina — preoccupied with personal performance metrics
jokes.explanation: A perfectly structured character revelation. Selina builds to what sounds like her most politically self-aware moment in the episode — then immediately wants to know her mile time. It's both funny and deeply true to who she is: the grand political vision and the petty personal vanity coexist without contradiction. 'That' as a sentence fragment at the end is a perfect comedic period. High character integration — this could only be Selina.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Hey, ma'am. I have some sort of hazy memory of me promising to buy your boat.' [pause] 'Yeah, you did. Well remembered.' Selina: 'That's not gonna happen. You can't hold me to that, right?' Mike: 'Yeah, no. Sure. Of course.'
jokes.setup: Selina remembers the boat deal from when she was high
jokes.punchline: She immediately reneges with casual confidence; Mike's 'Yeah, no. Sure. Of course' — agreeing while clearly devastated — is the punchline
jokes.explanation: The callback to the boat deal lands well. Mike's 'Yeah, no. Sure. Of course' is a beautifully defeated series of affirmations — he knows she's wrong, he agrees anyway, and his devastation is complete in five words. The setup has been building since the drug sequence.

jokes.text: 'Man, that is low. That's like trying to have sex with somebody who's passed out.'
jokes.setup: Mike has just had his boat deal canceled
jokes.punchline: Mike's comparison of Selina reneging on a deal made while high to sexual assault is a dark-subversive joke that reveals Mike's actual moral calculus — this is as bad as it gets for him
jokes.explanation: The dark comparison is very Mike — his outrage is genuine but his metaphor is both disproportionate and accidentally revealing. The show gets away with this because Mike's devastation is sympathetic.

jokes.text: Gary: 'Hey, ma'am. About my parents' party...' Selina: 'What? I thought your parents were dead.'
jokes.setup: Gary is about to follow up on the party invitation
jokes.punchline: Selina has zero memory of the conversation and, more importantly, has apparently always assumed Gary's parents were dead
jokes.explanation: The episode's best callback payoff. The setup has been running for 18 minutes — Gary's vulnerability, his validation needs, his father issues — and it pays off with Selina not remembering any of it AND revealing she'd always assumed his parents were dead. The 'I've just always assumed that they were dead' line is devastating: it tells us she has never thought about Gary's family life, ever. Gary's 'No. No? No.' is heartbreaking and funny simultaneously. The final MIKE line 'I thought you were adopted' piles on perfectly. This is Veep at its peak: comedy that's genuinely a little cruel.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I mean, why wouldn't I make that assumption? Why wouldn't...' Gary: 'Right. Yeah, totally. Yeah.' Selina: 'All right, let's back up for a second here. Your parents are not dead.' Gary: 'Nope. Mmm-mmm.' Selina: 'It's so funny that you thought that they were, too.'
jokes.setup: Selina rationalizes her assumption that Gary's parents were dead
jokes.punchline: She eventually checks in — 'Your parents are not dead' — as if confirming a new fact, and then adds 'It's so funny that you thought that they were, too' — projecting the error back onto Gary
jokes.explanation: 'It's so funny that you thought that they were, too' — Selina deflecting her own assumption onto Gary is extraordinarily specific Selina character writing. She neither apologizes nor acknowledges her error; she re-frames it as a shared delusion that Gary also had. Gary's 'Right. Yeah, totally. Yeah.' accepting this framing is the Gary/Selina dynamic in miniature. Exceptional character integration. One of the two best jokes in the episode alongside joke #101.

jokes.text: Mike's final line: 'I thought you were adopted.'
jokes.setup: The revelation that nobody knew anything about Gary's actual family
jokes.punchline: Mike's contribution — 'I thought you were adopted' — adds a third assumption about Gary's non-existent background, escalating the comedy of the group's complete indifference to his life
jokes.explanation: The perfect button to the episode. Four words that add a third layer — not just Selina thought his parents were dead, but Mike thought Gary had no biological family at all. The escalation is economical. Great final laugh.

tmdb_overview: Despite being told to limit public appearances, Selina continues her plans; Dan tries to play both sides of a rivalry.
insight: Campaign chaos hits 100 jokes in 43 minutes—Veep's densest episode yet.
